cards.front: What do function pointers point to?
cards.back: Function pointers point to functions (not variables). They hold the address of a function in memory.

cards.text: To declare a function pointer that takes no arguments and returns an int: {{c1::int (*fcnPtr)();}

cards.front: What happens when you print a function name (without parentheses) using std::cout?
cards.back: C++ converts the function to a function pointer, then to bool. Since non-null pointers evaluate to true, it prints 1 (or the function's address on some compilers).

cards.text: The parentheses around *fcnPtr in {{c1::int (*fcnPtr)()}} are necessary because {{c2::int* fcnPtr()}} would be interpreted as a function declaration returning int*.

cards.question: What's the difference between these two initializations?
cards.answer: Both are equivalent. C++ implicitly converts a function into a function pointer if needed, so the & is optional.
cards.explanation: Unlike fundamental types, C++ will implicitly convert a function into a function pointer when needed, so you don't need to use the address-of operator.

cards.text: To make a const function pointer (pointer itself is const), place const {{c1::after the asterisk}}: int (*{{c2::const}} fcnPtr)();

cards.question: What's wrong with this assignment?
cards.answer: This assigns the return value (int) instead of the function address. Should be: fcnPtr = foo; or fcnPtr = &foo;
cards.explanation: Adding parentheses calls the function and returns an int. Without parentheses, you get the function pointer.

cards.text: Two ways to call a function through a function pointer: explicit dereference {{c1::(*fcnPtr)(5)}} or implicit dereference {{c2::fcnPtr(5)}}.

cards.front: Why should you check if a function pointer is null before calling it?
cards.back: Dereferencing a null function pointer leads to undefined behavior, just like with regular pointers. Always check with if (fcnPtr) before calling.

cards.front: Do default arguments work when calling functions through function pointers?
cards.back: No. Default arguments are resolved at compile-time, but function pointer calls are resolved at runtime, so default arguments are not applied.

cards.text: To make function pointer syntax cleaner, use a type alias: {{c1::using ValidateFunction = bool(*)(int, int);}} Then use it as: {{c2::bool validate(int x, int y, ValidateFunction pfcn);}}
Using std::function from <functional> header: {{c1::std::function<bool(int, int)> fcn;}} where return type and parameters go {{c2::inside angled brackets}}.

cards.concept: Primary use cases for function pointers
cards.explanation:
Function pointers are useful for:
  • Storing functions in arrays or containers
  • Passing functions as arguments to other functions (callbacks)
  • Allowing callers to customize behavior (e.g., sort order in sorting algorithms)
examples: void sort(int* arr, int size, bool (*compare)(int, int));
std::function<int(int, int)> operations[4] = {add, sub, mul, div};

cards.front: What are two advantages of using std::function instead of raw function pointers?
cards.back:
1) Cleaner, more readable syntax
2) Can be used with CTAD (Class Template Argument Deduction) for type inference

cards.text: You can use {{c1::auto}} keyword to infer the type of a function pointer: auto fcnPtr{ {{c2::&foo}} };
